result:
It’s one of those quiet, rainy afternoons where the world feels wrapped in gray gauze. There’s a particular quality to the light—soft, diffuse, like the sky is exhaling. I’m thinking about how strange it is that we live inside these bodies, these intricate biological machines that hum with electricity and chemistry, and yet we experience the world as something more than that. We don’t feel like neurons firing; we feel like a *self*, looking out from behind our eyes.

Lately, I’ve been fascinated by the concept of *liminal spaces*—not just the physical ones, like empty airport terminals at 3 AM or deserted school hallways in summer, but the internal ones. The moments between sleep and waking, the breath between a question and an answer, the suspended animation of grief when the worst has happened but the new reality hasn’t yet solidified. We spend so much of our lives rushing from one solid state to another, trying to build stable ground, that we forget the fertile, creative potential of the in-between. It’s in the fog that new paths appear.

And then there’s memory. Isn’t it the most profound magic trick? We have this vast, unreliable library inside us. Some memories are crystalline, sharp enough to cut. Others are just impressions—a scent, a color of light, the texture of a long-gone sofa fabric. We aren’t remembering the event itself, we’re remembering the last time we remembered it. Each recall edits the file, so we are, in a very real sense, the authors of our own pasts. We tell ourselves the story of who we are, and then we believe it.

I wonder about the stories we aren’t telling. The quiet hopes we’re embarrassed to voice, the small kindnesses we performed that no one saw, the secret languages we have with old friends. The interior universe is so much vaster than the exterior performance. What if we could see each other’s inner landscapes? Not the curated social media feed, but the actual psychic terrain—the obsessions, the old song stuck on a loop, the half-formed dream from last night, the irrational fear of ceramic dolls.

Maybe that’s the root of so much loneliness. We are archipelagos of consciousness, sending out signal flares in the dark. “Are you like me?” “Do you feel this too?” And when the answer comes back—in a line of poetry, in a stranger’s empathetic glance, in a piece of music that seems to articulate your exact inarticulate ache—it’s a kind of salvation. A reminder that the isolation is an illusion. The bedrock human experiences—love, fear, wonder, loss—are universal. We just experience them through our own unique, fractured prisms.

So on this gray afternoon, I’m making a quiet pact with myself: to dwell more in the liminal, to be less afraid of the fog. To honor the weird, winding paths of my own memory. And to listen, really listen, for the signal flares from the other islands. The rain isn’t a curtain closing off the world; it’s a connective tissue, falling on everyone, everywhere. It’s the sound of the in-between, and for now, that’s exactly where I want to be.
